rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, July 9, 2019, Line 4, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication Defendant City and County of San Francisco's motion for summary judgment is denied; its alternative motion for summary adjudication is granted as to the third cause of action for wrongful death and negligence, and is denied as to the first and second causes of action arising under 42 U.S.C. § 1983 and the Eighth and Fourteenth Amendments. Plaintiff Janet M. Planells has conceded that the City is entitled to summary adjudication of the third cause of action on the grounds of statutory immunity. (Gov. rulings.Case Title: JANET M. PLANELLS VS. STATE OF CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, July 9, 2019, Line 4, DEFENDANT HEALTHRIGHT 360, A NONPROFIT CORPORATION'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or Summary Adjudication Defendant HealthRIGHT 360's motion for summary judgment is denied. Its alternative motion for summary adjudication is granted as to the third cause of action for negligence and wrongful death because Plaintiff has not presented any expert testimony to dispute HealthRIGHT 360's showing, through the Declaration of James H. Bramson, Psy.D., that Dr. Cohen's conduct was within the applicable standard of care. (See Scott v. Rayhrer (2010) 185 Cal.App.4th 1535, 1542 ["As a general rule, the testimony of an expert witness is required in every professional negligence case to establish the applicable standard of care, whether that standard was met or breached by the defendant, and whether any negligence by the defendant caused the plaintiff's damages"].) **The Courts full tentative ruling has been emailed to all counsel. ** Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: OREGON MUTUAL INSURANCE COMPANY VS. BRUCE I. GILBERT ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Amended Notice Of Motion Of Defendant Sunset Scavenger Company To Compel Further Responses To Form And Special Interrogatories; Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, July 09, 2019, Line 2, DEFENDANT SUNSET SCAVENGER COMPANY's Motion To Compel Further Responses To Form And Special Interrogatories; Request For Sanctions. (part 1 of 2) Pro Tem Judge Roger Mead, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The parties did not adequately meet and confer. Obregon v. Superior Court (1998) 67 CA4th 424. Had the parties engaged in substantive discussions, then the technical deficiencies/ambiguities of Sentinel's responses would likely have been remedied without need for a motion. SROGs 4, 27, 41-44, 46-47: A fair reading of Sentinel's responses (which could have been confirmed if the parties had adequately met and conferred) is that Sentinel does not itself have responsive information and will be relying on evidence obtained from Sunset or others with direct knowledge. "If the information which is sought is not in the possession of the party served or of its agent, then it is a sufficient answer to indicate that the information is unavailable or to refer to the person or entity which can provide the information." Pantzalas v. Superior Court (1969) 272 Cal.App.2d 499, 503; "[t]he purpose of the discovery statutes is to enable a party to obtain evidence under the control of his adversary in order to further the efficient and economical disposition of a lawsuit." Deyo v. Kilbourne, (1978) 84 Cal. App. 3d 771, 793 (emphasis added). FROGs 7.2, 7.3, 9.1: Sunset presents no evidence from which the Court could conclude that the reference to a 69 page "damages package" and 467 pages of "claim documents" is not sufficient "to permit the propounding party to locate and to identify, as readily as the responding party can, the documents from which the answer may be ascertained." CCP § 2030.230. FROG 12.3: Sentinel cites no authority suggesting that a party may withhold the identity of known potential witnesses that it has interviewed simply because it chose to have the interviews conducted by its expert. Motion denied except re FROG 12.3. Sentinel to provide amended response in 14 days that identifies individuals from whom statements have been obtained, the individual(s) who obtained such statements, the date each statement was obtained, and the persons who have the original or a copy of such statements. Nothing herein should be construed as requiring the production of any expert work product prior to the time for such production set forth in the CCP. Sanctions denied. (end of part 1 of 2) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: TERI FOWLER VS. REBECCA MENDRIBIL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Rebecca Mendribil, Alisa Mendribil, John Mendribil Amended Notice Of Defendants Special Motion To Strike (Part 2 Of 2 Tentative Ruling Purposes Only)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, July 9, 2019, Line 16, DefendantS Rebecca Mendribil, Alisa Mendribil, John Mendribil'S Amended Notice Of Defendants Special Motion To Strike (Part 2 Of 2 Tentative Ruling Purposes Only) Plaintiff has failed to show that the underlying action was initiated or maintained without probable cause. To make a prima facie case of lack of probable cause in response to a special motion to strike, the plaintiff must submit substantial evidence showing that no reasonable attorney would have thought the defendants' action against the plaintiff was tenable in light of the facts the defendants knew when suit was filed. (Nunez v. Pennisi (2015) 241 Cal.App.4th 861, 875-876.) "A litigant will lack probable cause for his action either if he relies upon facts which he has no reasonable cause to believe to be true, or if he seeks recovery upon a legal theory which is untenable under the facts known to him." (Sycamore Ridge Apartments, LLC v. Naumann (2007) 157 Cal.App.4th 1385, 1402.) While Plaintiff attacks the legal tenability of the theories of liability alleged in Defendants' verified pleadings, she has made no showing that the facts upon which they were based were false and known to be false by the parties to the underlying action or their attorney. Further, the challenge to the legal tenability of the claims fails because the trial court overruled a demurrer to the first amended complaint in the underlying action as to all causes of action and found that they were adequately pled. (RNJ , Exh. H; see Franklin Mint Co. v. Manatt, Phelps & Phillips, LLP (2010) 184 Cal.App.4th 313, 364 ["an order overruling a demurrer-or, in federal practice, denying a motion to dismiss-establishes as a matter of law the legal tenability of a cause of action as alleged. Because such an order does not address the evidence, however, it does not establish the factual tenability of the claim"].) Finally, Plaintiff has not shown a probability that Defendants initiated or maintained the underlying action with malice, i.e., out of ill will or for an improper purpose. (Soukup v. Law Offices of Herbert Hafif (2006) 39 Cal.4th 260, 292 ["The plaintiff must plead and prove actual ill will or some improper ulterior motive."].) The failed settlement discussions do not show that Defendants' settlement offer was made for purposes unrelated to the merits of the case, nor do Defendants' withdrawn discovery requests. (Rebecca Mendribil Decl. 15, Exhs. D, F.) The evidence shows that Defendants' delay in propounding discovery was for the purposes of cost savings pending resolution of the Arizona action and does not demonstrate "a degree of indifference from which one could infer malice." (Rebecca Mendribil Decl. 6, 7; see Medley Capital Corp. v. Security National Guaranty, Inc. (2017) 17 Cal.App.5th 33, 49.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. (end of part 2 of 2) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: TERI FOWLER VS. REBECCA MENDRIBIL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Amended Notice Of Defendants Special Motion To Strike
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, July 9, 2019, Line 16, DEFENDANT REBECCA MENDRIBIL, ALISA MENDRIBIL, JOHN MENDRIBIL'S Amended Notice Of Defendants Special Motion To Strike (part 1 of 2) Defendants Rebecca Mendribil, Alisa Mendribil, and John Mendribil's special motion to strike complaint pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure § 425.16 is granted. It is undisputed that the requirements of prong one are met because the challenged single cause of action arises out of defendants' protected activity - the filing of a lawsuit. (See Daniels v. Robbins (2010) 182 Cal.App.4th 204, 214-215 ["The plain language of the anti-SLAPP statute dictates that every claim of malicious prosecution is a cause of action arising from protected activity because every such claim necessarily depends upon written and oral statements in a prior judicial proceeding."].) Plaintiff has failed to meet her burden of showing minimal merit as to all the elements of her malicious prosecution action. (See Soukup v. Law Offices of Herbert Hafif (2006) 39 Cal.4th 260, 292 ["To prevail on a malicious prosecution claim, the plaintiff must show that the prior action (1) was commenced by or at the direction of the defendant and was pursued to a legal termination favorable to the plaintiff; (2) was brought without probable cause; and (3) was initiated with malice"].) Although Plaintiff has shown a likelihood of prevailing on the first element of favorable termination, she has failed to demonstrate a prima facie case as to the elements of lack of probable cause and malice. Because Plaintiff was voluntarily dismissed from the underlying action with prejudice and Defendants' evidence fails to establish as a matter of law that the dismissal was for reasons other than belief in the lack of lack of liability of Plaintiff, Plaintiff has made the requisite showing on this element. Unlike in Villa v. Cole (1992) 4 Cal.App.4th 1327, 1336, not only was Ms. Fowler expressly excluded from the settlement, but the settlement provided that notwithstanding the dismissal of the San Francisco action, the settling defendants agreed to cooperate with discovery already issued and discovery that may be forthcoming in relation to the causes of action against Terry Fowler. (Settlement F; 9). While Plaintiff argues that her pending summary judgment motion was the reason for her dismissal, Defendants submit declarations averring that after the settlement, pursuing the action against Plaintiff was not cost effective. (Rebecca Mendribil Decl. 11; Alisa Mendribil Decl. 7) Defendants' evidence does not defeat Plaintiff's showing as a matter of law. (See Oviedo v. Windsor Twelve Properties, LLC (2012) 212 Cal.App.4th 97, 112 ["The parties do not dispute that respondents filed a request for and received a voluntary dismissal without prejudice in the unlawful detainer action. Thus, it is possible to infer that the action was terminated favorably to appellant"]; Sycamore Ridge Apartments, LLC v. Naumann (2007) 157 Cal.App.4th 1385, 1400 ["A voluntary dismissal is presumed to be a favorable termination on the merits, unless otherwise proved to a jury"]; Medley Capital Corp. v. Security National Guaranty, Inc. (2017) 17 Cal.App.5th 33, 47 ["And should there be a conflict as to the circumstances of the termination, "the determination of the reasons underlying the dismissal is a question of fact"].) (end of part 1 of 2) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: MILENA LEVITIN VS. PHUKAB MONTAKARN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, July 9, 2019, Line 9, DEFENDANTS PHUKAB MONTAKARN, PRASOMSUK LLC, SIAM ORCHID TRADITIONAL THAI MASSAGE'S DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT Defendants Montakarn Phukab, Prasomsuk LLC, and Siam Orchid Traditional Thai Massage's demurrer to Plaintiff Milena Levitin's first amended complaint (FAC) is sustained without leave to amend as to Ms. Phukab and overruled as to Prasomsuk LLC and Siam Orchid. On demurrer, the Court accepts as true properly pled allegations of fact in the complaint as well as facts which may be inferred from those expressly alleged. (Mez Industries, Inc. v. Pacific Nat. Ins. Co. (1999) 76 Cal.App.4th 856.) Defendants improperly allege facts that are not found in the FAC and therefore those facts may not be considered. A demurrer for uncertainty is only sustained where a defendant cannot reasonably understand what claims are directed against him or her. (Khoury v. Maly's of California, Inc. (1993) 14 Cal.App.4th 612, 616.) Plaintiff alleges in the FAC that DOE 1 failed to render massage services with reasonable care and that as a result she sustained injuries to her shoulders, upper back, and neck which required medical treatment. Thus, she has sufficiently pled the elements of duty, breach, causation, and damages as required for a negligence cause of action. (See Wise v. Superior Court (1990) 222 Cal.App.3d 1008, 1013.) However, Ms. Phukab may not be sued in her individual capacity under these circumstances. Vicarious liability, agency liability, and negligent hiring liability theories run to the employer, Siam Orchid, rather than to an individual such as Ms. Phukab. (See Civ. Code § 2338 [establishing employer liability for negligence of employees in scope of employment]; C.A. v. William S. Hart Union High School Dist. (2012) 53 Cal.4th 861, 877 ["Absent . . . a special relationship, there can be no individual liability to third parties for negligent hiring, retention or supervision of a fellow employee"]; Roman Catholic Bishop v. Superior Court (1996) 42 Cal.App.4th 1556, 1564 ["An employer may be liable to a third person for the employer's negligence in hiring or retaining an employee who is incompetent or unfit."] [emphasis added].) There are no allegations to suggest that Ms. Phukab should be individually liable for the alleged negligence of DOE 1, and Siam Orchid should adequately satisfy the policy behind negligent hiring liability. (See Phillips v. TLC Plumbing, Inc. (2009) 172 Cal.App.4th 1133, 1139 ["Liability for negligent hiring ... is based upon the reasoning that if an enterprise hires individuals with characteristics which might pose a danger to customers or other employees, the enterprise should bear the loss caused by the wrongdoing of its incompetent or unfit employees."].) Moreover, to the extent Prasomsuk LLC is liable as a successor that assumed all past, present, and future liability of the business, it is solely liable to the exclusion of individual officers such as Ms. Phukab. (See Corp. Code § 17703.04(a)(1) [liabilities of LLC are solely those of the LLC].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: GLENN MAHLER ET AL VS. JUDICIAL COUNCIL OF CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Preliminary Injunction Relating To The Assigned Judges Program
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, July 9, 2019, Line 13, PLAINTIFFS GLENN MAHLER, JAMES POOLE, JULIE CONGER, EDWARD LACY JR., WILLIAMS LEBOV, JOHN MINNEY, JOHN SAPUNOR, F. SUEYRES' Motion For Preliminary Injunction Relating To The Assigned Judges Program Plaintiffs are eight retired Superior Court judges who are dissatisfied with recent changes in the eligibility requirements for the Temporary Assigned Judges Program (TAJP) implemented by Defendants the Judicial Council of California and Chief Justice Tani G. Cantil-Sakauye, in her official capacity as the chair of the Judicial Council. They seek a preliminary injunction enjoining Defendants from retroactively enforcing one of those changes, which with certain exceptions limits participation in that program to a lifetime total of 1,320 working days, the equivalent of six years, the term of a full-time, elected superior court judge. For the following reasons, Plaintiffs' motion for a preliminary injunction is denied. **The Court's full tentative ruling has been emailed to all counsel** Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)
